title: Flapjacks
subtitle: Golden oat traybake
excerpt: A four-ingredient traybake: rolled oats bound with butter, brown sugar, and golden syrup, pressed into a tin and baked until the edges caramelise. Cut while warm, cool in the tin.
sourceNotes: Flapjacks are a British oat traybake distinct from the American pancake. The combination of rolled oats, golden syrup, and butter appears in British household cookery from the early 20th century and became a wartime staple in the 1940s. Period recipes are documented in Ministry of Food publications and post-war British cookery guides.

content.text: Flapjacks have no flour and no egg. The oats are coated in a hot mixture of butter, sugar, and golden syrup and baked until the edges caramelise and the centre firms. The ratio matters: more butter gives a crumblier result; more syrup gives a chewier one. This recipe lands in the middle. The
is narrow; cut as soon as the tray has rested for 10 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease and line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Melt the butter, sugar, golden syrup, and salt together in a saucepan over low heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and stir in the oats until every grain is coated. Tip into the tin and press firmly into an even layer with the back of a spoon. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the edges are deep golden brown and the centre has set. Remove from the oven, leave for 10 minutes, then cut into 16 bars while still warm. Leave in the tin to cool completely before removing.
